title: Amazon Bedrock Introduces Advanced Prompt Optimization and Migration Tool
description:
Customers spend days to weeks optimizing prompts and evaluating responses when they want to migrate to a new model or just get better performance out of their current model. They struggle with changing their prompts quickly and then testing them to prevent regressions and improve on underperforming tasks. These situations call for the same tool – a prompt optimizer with built-in evaluations. 

Today, Amazon Bedrock introduces Advanced Prompt Optimization, a new tool that allows customers to optimize their prompts for any model on Bedrock, while comparing their original prompts to their optimized prompts across up to 5 models simultaneously. Customers can use this if they are migrating to a new model or just want to get better performance on their current model. If they’re changing models, they can select their current model as a baseline and up to 4 other models. If they aren’t changing models, they just select their current model to see before and after optimization. The optimizer takes in prompt templates, example user inputs for the variable values, optional ground truth answers, and an evaluation metric or short natural language criteria to use as a guide. It's even compatible with multimodal inputs such as jpg, png, or PDF. The prompt optimizer works in a feedback loop to steer the prompt and resulting model responses toward optimizing the evaluation metric, and outputs the original and final prompt templates with evaluation scores, cost estimates, and latency.

For region availability, see our documentation. For pricing, see the Bedrock pricing page. To get started, use the Bedrock APIs for Advanced Prompt Optimizer or visit the Bedrock Console.

title: Automation Showdown: n8n vs. Zapier vs. Make - Choosing Your Workflow Engine
description:
Automation Showdown: n8n vs. Zapier vs. Make - Choosing Your Workflow Engine

In today's interconnected digital landscape, the ability to automate tasks and streamline workflows is no longer a luxury; it's a necessity. Businesses and individuals alike are constantly seeking ways to reduce manual effort, improve efficiency, and unlock new possibilities through integration. This has led to the rise of powerful workflow automation platforms.

Among the most prominent players in this space are Zapier, Make (formerly Integromat), and n8n. While all three aim to connect disparate applications and automate processes, they approach this goal with different philosophies, feature sets, and pricing models. This article will provide a comprehensive comparison to help you navigate this decision and choose the platform best suited for your needs.

Understanding the Core Concepts

Before diving into the specifics, let's clarify some fundamental concepts:

  • Integrations/Connectors: These are pre-built connections that allow a workflow automation platform to communicate with specific applications (e.g., Google Sheets, Slack, Mailchimp).
  • Triggers: An event in one application that initiates a workflow. For example, a new email arriving in Gmail could be a trigger.
  • Actions: A task performed in another application as a result of a trigger. For example, creating a new row in a Google Sheet after a new email is received.
  • Workflows/Scenarios/Workflows: The sequence of triggers and actions that define an automated process. The terminology varies between platforms.
  • Data Transformation: The ability to manipulate or reformat data as it flows between applications.
  • Logic and Branching: The capacity to create conditional pathways within a workflow, enabling different actions based on specific data conditions.

Zapier: The User-Friendly Giant

Zapier is arguably the most well-known and widely adopted automation platform. Its strength lies in its remarkable ease of use and extensive library of integrations.

Key Features and Strengths:

  • Vast Integration Library: Zapier boasts over 6,000+ integrations, covering a wide spectrum of popular SaaS applications. If an app has an API, there's a high chance Zapier has a pre-built integration for it.
  • Intuitive Interface: Its "Zap" builder uses a clear, step-by-step wizard that is incredibly beginner-friendly. You select a trigger app, the trigger event, an action app, and the action event, then map fields.
  • Simple Logic and Filtering: Zapier offers built-in "Filters" and "Formatters" to introduce basic logic and data manipulation, such as only proceeding if a field contains specific text or converting data formats.
  • Robust Community and Support: With its large user base, Zapier has extensive documentation, tutorials, and active community forums.
  • Task-Based Pricing: Pricing is based on the number of "Tasks" (each step in a Zap) executed per month and the frequency of your Zaps.

Potential Drawbacks:

  • Complexity Limitations: While Zapier excels at simple to moderately complex workflows, advanced logic, intricate branching, and multi-step data transformations can become cumbersome or impossible to achieve within its standard interface.
  • Cost at Scale: For high-volume workflows or those requiring many complex steps, Zapier's task-based pricing can become expensive.
  • Less Control over Infrastructure: Zapier is a fully managed SaaS solution, offering less control over where your data is processed or the underlying infrastructure.

Example:

Let's say you want to automatically add new Google Forms responses to a Google Sheet and send a Slack notification.

  1. Trigger: Google Forms - "New Response"
  2. Action 1: Google Sheets - "Add Row" (mapping form fields to sheet columns)
  3. Action 2: Slack - "Send Channel Message" (customizing message with form data)

Zapier guides you through each step visually, making it straightforward to set up.

Make (formerly Integromat): The Visual Powerhouse

Make stands out with its highly visual and flexible scenario builder. It provides a more powerful and customizable approach to workflow automation, particularly for complex integrations.

Key Features and Strengths:

  • Visual Drag-and-Drop Interface: Make's core strength is its intuitive visual builder, where you connect modules (representing apps and operations) in a flow-chart-like manner. This makes complex workflows easier to visualize and manage.
  • Advanced Logic and Branching: Make offers sophisticated tools for conditional logic, branching, aggregations, and error handling. You can create intricate pathways, iterate through data arrays, and implement complex decision-making within your scenarios.
  • Powerful Data Transformation: Built-in functions and operators allow for extensive data manipulation, enabling you to transform, filter, and combine data in virtually any way required.
  • Modular Design: Scenarios are built from interconnected modules, providing excellent reusability and a clear understanding of data flow.
  • Webhooks and API Handling: Make has robust support for webhooks and interacting directly with APIs, making it suitable for custom integrations.
  • Usage-Based Pricing: Pricing is based on the number of "Operations" (each executed function within a scenario) and the "Data Out" volume.

Potential Drawbacks:

  • Steeper Learning Curve: While visually appealing, the depth of functionality means Make can have a steeper learning curve than Zapier, especially for users new to automation.
  • Integration Depth (Compared to Zapier): While Make has a substantial and growing number of integrations, it may not match Zapier's sheer volume for every niche application. However, its flexibility often compensates for this.
  • Potential for Over-Engineering: The power of Make can sometimes lead to over-engineered solutions if not carefully planned.

Example:

Imagine a scenario where a new order is placed in Shopify. You want to:

  1. Create a customer record in your CRM.
  2. If the order total is over $100, create a task in your project management tool for a follow-up call.
  3. Send a confirmation email to the customer.

Make's visual builder allows you to:

  1. Trigger: Shopify - "New Order"
  2. Module 1: CRM - "Create Contact" (mapping customer details)
  3. Router Module: This allows for branching.
    • Condition: Order total > 100
      • Action: Project Management Tool - "Create Task"
    • Else: (No action needed for this branch)
  4. Action: Email Service - "Send Email" (customizing with order details)

This scenario clearly illustrates Make's ability to handle conditional logic and multi-step processes visually.

n8n.io: The Open-Source Innovator

n8n presents a compelling alternative, particularly for users who value flexibility, control, and cost-effectiveness. It champions an open-source model with a self-hostable option.

Key Features and Strengths:

  • Open-Source and Self-Hostable: This is n8n's defining characteristic. You can run it on your own servers, providing complete data privacy and control. This also translates to significant cost savings, especially for extensive usage.
  • Visual Workflow Builder: Similar to Make, n8n offers a visual drag-and-drop interface for building workflows, making it intuitive to design complex automations.
  • Extensive Node Library: n8n provides a rich set of "Nodes" (similar to modules in Make) for various applications and functionalities, including custom HTTP requests, database operations, and JavaScript code execution.
  • Powerful Data Handling and Transformations: n8n excels at transforming data and offers advanced features like expression editing and the ability to write custom JavaScript code directly within workflows for ultimate flexibility.
  • Version Control and Collaboration: Its design facilitates versioning of workflows, aiding in development and collaboration.
  • Generous Free Tier (Cloud): Even the cloud version offers a substantial free tier, making it accessible for individuals and small projects.

Potential Drawbacks:

  • Self-Hosting Complexity: While powerful, self-hosting requires technical expertise for setup, maintenance, and scaling.
  • Integration Breadth (Compared to Zapier): While n8n's integration library is rapidly growing, it may not be as exhaustive as Zapier's for some niche applications. However, its ability to make raw HTTP requests and execute custom code mitigates this significantly.
  • Community Support (Newer Platform): While the community is active and growing, it's newer than Zapier's, so the breadth of readily available solutions might be smaller.

Example:

Consider a scenario where you receive data from an external API, need to process it using a custom script, and then store it in a PostgreSQL database.

  1. Node 1: HTTP Request - Fetch data from the external API.
  2. Node 2: Code - A JavaScript node to process and transform the fetched data.
  3. Node 3: PostgreSQL - "Insert Row" (mapping the transformed data to database columns).

The ability to embed custom JavaScript here provides unparalleled control over data manipulation, which is a significant advantage.

Feature Comparison Table

Feature Zapier Make (formerly Integromat) n8n.io
Interface Step-by-step wizard Visual drag-and-drop Visual drag-and-drop
Complexity Simple to moderate Moderate to complex Moderate to highly complex
Logic & Branching Basic filters and conditions Advanced branching, conditional logic Advanced branching, conditional logic
Data Transformation Basic formatting Powerful built-in functions Powerful functions, custom JavaScript
Integrations 6,000+ (extensive) Growing library, very flexible Growing library, highly extensible
Custom Code Limited (via Formatter) Limited (via specific modules) Built-in JavaScript editor
Hosting Cloud (SaaS) Cloud (SaaS) Cloud (SaaS) or Self-hosted
Pricing Model Task-based Operation-based Free tier, Paid tiers, Self-hosted (free)
Learning Curve Low Medium Medium (steeper if self-hosting)
Data Privacy Managed by Zapier Managed by Make High (especially with self-hosting)

Which Platform is Right for You?

Choosing the right platform depends heavily on your specific needs, technical proficiency, budget, and the complexity of your desired automations.

  • Choose Zapier if:

    • You are new to workflow automation and prioritize ease of use.
    • You need to connect to a vast array of popular applications quickly.
    • Your workflows are relatively straightforward and don't require complex logic or extensive data manipulation.
    • Budget is less of a concern for moderate automation needs.

  • Choose Make if:

    • You have moderately to highly complex workflows with intricate logic and branching requirements.
    • You appreciate a visual, flow-chart-like approach to building automation.
    • You need to perform advanced data transformations.
    • You are comfortable with a slightly steeper learning curve for greater power.

  • Choose n8n.io if:

    • Data privacy and control are paramount, and you prefer self-hosting.
    • You require maximum flexibility and the ability to write custom code within your workflows.
    • You are looking for a cost-effective solution for extensive or high-volume automation.
    • You have the technical expertise to manage a self-hosted solution or are comfortable with their cloud offering.

Conclusion

Zapier, Make, and n8n represent distinct yet powerful approaches to workflow automation. Zapier shines with its accessibility and breadth of integrations, making it the go-to for quick, straightforward automations. Make offers a visually intuitive and robust platform for crafting complex, multi-step workflows with advanced logic. n8n, with its open-source nature and self-hosting capabilities, provides unparalleled control, flexibility, and cost-effectiveness for those who need it.

By understanding the strengths and weaknesses of each platform, you can make an informed decision that will empower your business or personal projects with efficient and intelligent automation. The key is to align the platform's capabilities with the complexity of your tasks and your organization's technical resources and budgetary constraints.

title: April 27, 2026
description:
API Gateway

Change

New validations on paths in API configurations

API Gateway now enforces stricter syntax validations on templated paths when you create new API configurations and gateways.

See path templating syntax rules and limits for more information.

Apigee hybrid

Announcement

v1.14.4

On April 27, 2026 we released an updated version of the Apigee hybrid software, v1.14.4.

  • For information on upgrading, see Upgrading Apigee hybrid to version v1.14.4.
  • For information on new installations, see The big picture.
Note: This is a patch release: The container images used in patch releases are integrated with the Apigee hybrid Helm charts. Upgrading to a patch via the Helm chart automatically updates the images. No manual image changes are typically needed. For information on container image support in Apigee hybrid releases, see Apigee release process.

Feature

Sidecar authentication for Workload Identity Federation on non-GKE platforms

Starting in version v1.14.4, you can now use a sidecar along with Workload Identity Federation on non-GKE platforms to mount security tokens from your preferred identity provider (IDP) for service account authentication. See Use sidecar authentication for Workload Identity Federation on non-GKE platforms.

Cloud Billing

Feature

The AI Cost Summary Agent is now available in Preview

You can now use the AI Cost Summary Agent to analyze your AI costs and gain critical insights into your AI-related spend. The agent analyzes spending related to Gemini usage, including Gemini API and Vertex AI.

This feature is available as a widget on the Billing Overview page for your Cloud Billing account.

For more information, see Analyze your AI spend with the AI Cost Summary Agent.

Cloud Load Balancing

Feature

A new quota system governing the configuration size of Application Load Balancer is now available in Preview. This update increases the individual URL map size limit from 64 KB and 128 KB to 1 MB. For more information, see URL map size and quota units.

Key aspects of this feature include:

  • Complexity-based quota: Quota units reflect URL map complexity (number of rules, hostnames, and path matchers).
  • Scoped measurement: Quota is measured and enforced on a per-project, per-region, or per-VPC depending on Application Load Balancer type.
  • Active consumption: Only URL maps currently referenced by forwarding rules contribute to quota usage.
  • New URL map size limit: Projects enabled for the new quota have a new URL map size limit increased to 1 MB for global and regional external and internal Application Load Balancers. Classic Application Load Balancer remain restricted to 64 KB.

For more information on increasing your limit or to participate in the preview, please contact Google Cloud Support.

Cloud Service Mesh

Announcement

Managed Cloud Service Mesh using the TRAFFIC_DIRECTOR implementation in the regular channel now supports a limited implementation of the EnvoyFilter API. To learn about the supported fields, extensions, and how to use EnvoyFilter for features like local rate limiting see Data plane extensibility with EnvoyFilter.

To troubleshoot any issue while configuring, see Resolving data plane extensibility issues.

Cloud Storage

Feature

Cloud Storage now offers support for AI zones. To learn more, see AI zones.

Cloud TPU

Feature

Generally available: Cloud TPU now offers TPU availability in AI zones. To learn more, see About AI zones.

Cloud Trace

Feature

Cloud Trace is a service covered by the Cloud Observability (Monitoring, Logging, Trace) Service Level Agreement (SLA).

Cloud Workstations

Feature

Cloud Workstations UI supports additional machine series and types, including A2, A3, C3, C4, G4, M3, N4, and Z3. You can also select Confidential C3 standard machine types. For more information, see Available machine types.

The preconfigured base images include a notification when the running_timeout for the workstation is close to being reached.

Compute Engine

Feature

Generally available: Compute Engine now offers support for AI zones. To learn more, see AI zones.

Google Cloud VMware Engine

Feature

Generally available (GA) with an allowlist: You can now migrate VMware management VMs from their host cluster to a different cluster within the same private cloud. This feature is available to select customers through an allowlist. To migrate management VMs, you must have at least two clusters in your private cloud, and the destination cluster must be a workload cluster. This operation transitions the target workload cluster to a management cluster, and the source management cluster becomes a workload cluster.

Google Kubernetes Engine

Feature

Google Kubernetes Engine now offers support for AI zones. To learn more, see AI zones.

Google SecOps

Announcement

New parser documentation now available

New parser documentation is available to help you ingest and normalize logs from the following sources:

  • Collect Group-IB Threat Intelligence logs
  • Collect Microsoft System Center Endpoint Protection (SCEP) logs
  • Collect Nagios XI logs
  • Collect Neo4j Aura logs
  • Collect Nucleus Security - Nucleus Unified Vulnerability Management logs
  • Collect Nyansa Voyance / VMware Edge Network Intelligence logs
  • Collect Okera Dynamic Access Platform (ODAP) audit logs
  • Collect Okta Advanced Server Access logs
  • Collect Onapsis Platform logs
  • Collect One Identity TPAM logs
  • Collect Oracle Cloud Infrastructure - Oracle Cloud Guard logs
  • Collect Cisco Identity Intelligence logs
  • Collect Microsoft SharePoint (Office 365) logs
  • Collect NetApp Console (formerly BlueXP) audit logs

Guest Environment

Fixed

Version 20260423.01 of the guest agent is now available for all supported operating systems. This version includes the following fixes:

  • Updates the guest agent Go dependencies to address security vulnerabilities.
  • Ensures that the internal initialization is complete before signaling readiness to systemd. This fix resolves an issue in version 20260329.00 where dependent services like SSH failed to start due to missing host keys.

title: Big Tech firms are accelerating AI investments and integration, while regulators and companies focus on safety and responsible adoption.
description:
The AI landscape is experiencing unprecedented growth and transformation. This post delves into the key developments shaping the future of artificial intelligence, from massive industry investments to critical safety considerations and integration into core development processes.

Key Areas Explored:

  • Record-Breaking Investments: Major tech firms are committing billions to AI infrastructure, signaling a significant acceleration in the field.
  • AI in Software Development: We examine how companies are leveraging AI for code generation and the implications for engineering workflows.
  • Safety and Responsibility: The increasing focus on ethical AI development and protecting vulnerable users, particularly minors.
  • Market Dynamics: How AI is influencing stock performance, cloud computing strategies, and global market trends.
  • Global AI Strategies: Companies are adapting AI development for specific regional markets.

This deep dive aims to provide developers, tech leaders, and enthusiasts with a comprehensive overview of the current state and future trajectory of AI.
